meta.description: Educational 'how people typically wheel this — and why / why-not' write-ups, one per name, grounded in the iv_data.json facts. Facts-first and descriptive; NOT advice.
meta.pairing: Join to iv_data.json on ticker. 'band' is the current-state grouping; 'writeup' is prose.
meta.disclaimer: Educational/informational only. NOT investment advice. May contain gaps or errors.

AAPL.writeup: **AAPL** — a mega-cap ($4.17T) consumer-tech company, and a frequent example of the "wheel a quality name you'd be content to own" approach. Measured implied vol ~24% (IV rank ~0.55, upper half of its range), realized ~22%, a thin ~1.3-point gap — single mega-caps are generally wheeled for the ownership and steadiness rather than a fat premium. The wheel runs as the standard single-name rotation — moderate-delta cash-secured puts, roll or take assignment, covered calls — with the recurring filter being "would I own this anyway." The structural risk for a single name is company event risk, above all earnings, and the facts show a name that has weathered it: the worst 1-year drawdown was −13.8% (recovered in 37 days) and the deepest 3–5-year episode −33.4% (recovered in 195 days); worst single day −9.2% (April 2025). Common practice treats assignment as acceptable here precisely because it's a mega-cap that has historically recovered. (Caveat: the snapshot ATM-put spread read wide, ~33% — an indicative, time-sensitive reading, not a verdict on AAPL's liquidity, which is among the deepest anywhere; recent volume ~2,600/day reflects active trading.)

DIA.writeup: ### DIA — broad-index ETF (Dow Jones Industrial Average)  DIA tracks the Dow Jones Industrial Average — 30 large, established US companies, price-weighted — so it's a diversified basket of blue chips, and the calmest of these four by the measured numbers. Its implied volatility is the lowest of the group, around **13%** (IV rank ~**0.25**), with realized vol about **12%** — a ~1.4-point gap, the smallest premium here. Common practice frames DIA much like SPY: a low-yield, low-maintenance core rotation.  The wheel mechanics are the standard broad-index approach — moderate-delta cash-secured puts, roll or take assignment, covered calls. Of these four, DIA's options are the least active: recent volume on the measured ATM put was about **140 contracts/day**, with a snapshot spread around **8%** (indicative). It trades, but a wheel on DIA typically involves the thinnest option activity of this cluster — which many traders weigh against SPY, whose nearly-identical large-cap exposure comes with far deeper option liquidity.  On risk, from the facts: DIA's measured drawdowns are the shallowest of the four but still real, and have recovered. The worst 1-year decline was about **−10%** (recovered in ~55 days), the 3-year about **−17%** (~136 days), and the deepest 5-year episode about **−22%**, recovered in roughly **439 trading days**. The worst single day was about **−5.4%**. As with the other index baskets, the historical recoverability is the reason assignment is typically treated as a wait rather than a loss.

F.writeup: **F (Ford)** — a large-cap ($56B) automaker, and the example where the fact profile carries a warning the recovered names don't. Measured IV ~34% (IV rank ~0.61, upper part of its range), realized ~29%, a ~4.5-point gap that looks like an attractive premium. The mechanics are standard, but the dominant consideration is the drawdown asymmetry: while the 1-year (−22%, recovered 45 days) and 3-year (−43%, recovered 414 days) declines recovered, the **5-year maximum drawdown was −65.5% and the stock is still ~44% below that prior peak** — it has not recovered. Worst single day −18.4%. That reshapes the "would I own it for years" question, because the realistic outcome of selling puts on a name like this is being assigned near a level it then sits below for an extended period. Common practice frames the premium as compensation for a low-recovery tail, and notes the covered-call phase can stall — once assigned underwater, selling calls at or above the higher cost basis collects little, while selling below it locks in a loss if called away. (Liquidity is workable: ~10% spread, ~1,265/day.)

IWM.writeup: ### IWM — broad-index ETF (Russell 2000 small-caps)  IWM tracks the Russell 2000 — ~2,000 small-cap US companies — so it's a broadly diversified basket, but of smaller, more volatile names than the large-cap indices. Its measured implied volatility is around **21%** (IV rank ~**0.33**), with realized vol about **19%** — a ~2-point gap, a modest premium. The higher IV than SPY or DIA reflects the small-cap composition rather than any single name.  The wheel runs on IWM the same way as the other index ETFs — moderate-delta cash-secured puts, roll or take assignment, sell covered calls — as a diversified rotation. Option activity is lighter than the headline large-cap ETFs: recent volume on the measured ATM put was about **460 contracts/day**, with a spread around **7%** on the snapshot (an indicative reading); an actively traded contract, just thinner than SPY or QQQ.  On risk, from the facts: IWM's drawdowns are real and have taken the longest to recover among these four. The worst 1-year decline was about **−11%** (recovered in ~17 days), the 3-year about **−28%** (~163 days), and the deepest 5-year episode about **−33%**, which took roughly **692 trading days — close to three years — to fully recover**. The worst single day was about **−6.4%**. The recoverability that makes assignment on a broad basket rarely a crisis still applies, but the measured recovery times here are notably longer, which many traders describe as the practical cost of the small-cap tilt.

QQQ.writeup: ### QQQ — broad-index ETF (Nasdaq-100)  QQQ tracks the Nasdaq-100 — ~100 of the largest non-financial names, heavily weighted toward technology — so it's a diversified basket, but a more concentrated, higher-volatility one than the broad S&P. Its measured implied volatility is around **18%**, and notably its IV rank is high (~**0.80**): current IV sits in the upper part of QQQ's own recent range even as the absolute level stays moderate. Realized vol is about **16%**, leaving a gap of ~2.7 points — a somewhat larger premium than the calmer index ETFs, reflecting the tech concentration. Traders who watch IV rank often describe premium-selling as more attractive when rank is elevated, treating the absolute level and the rank as separate signals.  The mechanics are the same broad-index rotation — moderate-delta cash-secured puts, roll or expire, take assignment, sell covered calls. Recent option volume is active (~**3,000 contracts/day**); the measured ATM-put spread read wider (~**16%**) on this snapshot, though that figure is an indicative-feed, time-sensitive reading rather than a verdict on QQQ's liquidity, which is among the deepest in the listed-options market.  On risk, from the facts: QQQ's drawdowns run deeper than the broad S&P, as the tech concentration implies, but have recovered. The worst 1-year decline was about **−12%** (recovered in ~16 days), the 3-year about **−23%** (~77 days), and the deepest 5-year episode about **−36%**, recovered in roughly **352 trading days**. The worst single day was about **−6.2%**. Common practice treats assignment on a diversified index as workable for the same reason as the broad market — historical recoverability — while noting the deeper drawdowns mean a potentially longer wait in shares.

SPY.writeup: ### SPY — broad-index ETF (S&P 500)  SPY tracks the S&P 500 — a basket of ~500 large US companies — so it's the canonical "core" wheel underlying, carrying market-level rather than single-company risk. Its measured implied volatility is low, around **13%** (IV rank ~**0.26**, toward the lower third of its own recent range), and realized vol is about **11%** — the ~1.7-point gap is the modest premium option sellers are typically compensated with here. Because the absolute premium is small, common practice describes the wheel on SPY as a low-yield, low-maintenance rotation rather than an income engine.  In practice, traders running the wheel on a broad index typically sell cash-secured puts a moderate distance out of the money on monthly or shorter expiries, roll or let them expire, and take assignment without much alarm. The contract people use is deeply liquid — the measured ATM-put spread was tight (~**0.9%**) with steady recent volume (~**5,800 contracts/day**) — so mechanical frictions are rarely the constraint; the small premium is.  On risk, the facts rather than the label: SPY's measured drawdowns are real but have recovered. The worst 1-year decline was about **−9%** (back to prior highs in ~**16** trading days), the 3-year about **−19%** (~80 days), and the deepest 5-year episode about **−25%**, which took roughly **464 trading days** to fully recover — but did recover. The worst single day in the multi-year window was about **−5.9%** (April 2025). Many traders cite that historical recoverability of a broad index as the reason assignment here is rarely a crisis — they expect to hold shares and sell covered calls until the position works back, rather than to be permanently impaired.

A short-term *hedge*, not a holding: −3× the Nasdaq's *daily* return, and a rising market plus daily-reset compounding grinds it toward zero. The −97.8% is the mechanism, doesn't come back by design. (worst day −35.3% on a big up-day.)

UVXY.writeup: **UVXY** — 1.5x short-term VIX-futures ETF. IV ~101% (rank ~0.15), realized ~78%, +22.7-pt gap. The drawdown facts *are* the product: **−99.7% over 5yr, still −99.7% underwater** (−94.9% 3yr, −72.7% 1yr) — never recovered at any horizon. Structural decay, not a dislocation: it holds VIX futures that bleed to roll-cost (contango) daily and reverse-splits to stay listed — **engineered to grind toward zero**. A user must not read −99.7% as a deep-value bounce; whatever the ~100% IV pays is dwarfed by the structural drift. (~25/day; worst day −31.6%.)
